provenance.source_corpus: Genetic Assessment of Walleye (Sander vitreus) Restoration Efforts and Options in Nipigon Bay and Black Bay, Lake Superior

source_spans.text: collapsed in the 1960s due to overfishing, habitat loss, and other pressures.
collapsed in the 1960s due to overfishing, habitat loss, and other pressures.

Walleye (Sander vitreus) stocks in Nipigon Bay and Black Bay historically numbered as the largest stocks in Lake Superior, but collapsed in the 1960s due to overfishing, habitat loss, and other pressures.
adult transfers from four source populations into Nipigon Bay differed substantially in their contributions to the re-established population.
adult transfers from four source populations into Nipigon Bay differed substantially in their contributions to the re-established population.
Rehabilitative stocking of early life stages of walleye into Nipigon Bay from 1978 to 1986 (Table 1) had little apparent success
Population assessment throughout the 1980s failed to detect significant numbers of walleye
given access to suitable restored habitat and release from fishing pressure, walleye are capable of some degree of recovery
given access to suitable restored habitat and release from fishing pressure, walleye are capable of some degree of recovery
given access to suitable restored habitat and release from fishing pressure, walleye are capable of some degree of recovery
Re-establishing connectivity among component habitats could greatly enhance walleye numbers in Black Bay
Re-establishing connectivity among component habitats could greatly enhance walleye numbers in Black Bay
